title: Relationship between doping-induced in-gap states and spin excitations in Kitaev-Hubbard models
authors: Si-Qi Hou, Shun-Li Yu, Zhao-Yang Dong, Jian-Xin Li
abstract: We investigate the connection between doping-induced in-gap states and underlying spin excitations in Mott insulators by employing cluster perturbation theory on one-dimensional (1D) and quasi-1D Kitaev-Hubbard models. By manipulating Kitaev-like hopping terms ($t^{\prime}$) that selectively control spin anisotropies in the strong-coupling limit, we establish a direct correspondence between the kinetic dispersion of the in-gap states and the spin excitation spectra. Specifically, in the Z chain, in-gap states evolve from a gapless dispersion to a gapped flat band as the system transitions from the Heisenberg to the Ising model, exhibiting a gap scaling of $2t^{\prime 2}/U$ that matches the Ising spin gap. In the XY chain, the in-gap states split into a dispersive and a flat branch at the Kitaev limit, perfectly mirroring the Jordan-Wigner fermionic spectrum. For the two-leg ladder, we observe an emergent broad continuum of in-gap states that reflects the fractionalization of spin excitations, accompanied by a gap manifesting the presence of topological $Z_2$ visons. Our results establish a robust correspondence between charge and spin dynamics in doped Mott insulators and demonstrate that in-gap states can serve as a probe of exotic quantum spin phenomena, including fractionalization and topological excitations, offering a new pathway to investigate spin liquids via spectroscopic probes of charge excitations.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the movement of electrons in doped Mott insulators (in-gap states) directly mirrors the underlying spin dynamics. By showing that charge spectroscopy can reveal exotic phenomena like Majorana fermions and Z2 visons, it provides a new way to study spin liquids using charge-based probes like ARPES or STM.
main_problem: Investigating the connection between doping-induced in-gap electronic states and underlying spin excitations in 1D and quasi-1D Kitaev-Hubbard models.
main_result: Established a direct correspondence between the kinetic dispersion of in-gap charge states and spin excitation spectra, demonstrating that in-gap states can serve as a spectroscopic probe for fractionalization and topological excitations.
method: Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT) combined with Exact Diagonalization (ED) and Jordan-Wigner transformation.
model_or_system: 1D and quasi-1D Kitaev-Hubbard models, including Z chains, XY chains, and two-leg ladders, operating in the strong-coupling (Mott insulator) limit.
key_observables: Single-particle spectral functions, kinetic dispersion of in-gap states, spin excitation spectra (transverse dynamic spin structure factor), and spectral weight transfer.
important_parameters: On-site Coulomb repulsion (U), Kitaev-like hopping amplitude (t'), and hole doping concentration (delta).
main_assumptions: Strong-coupling limit (U >> t, t') and the use of finite-size clusters to approximate infinite lattices.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the 12-site clusters used; Figure 4 shows the evolution of in-gap modes in the XY chain; Figure 5 displays spectral functions and spin structure factors for the two-leg ladder; Figure 6 shows the spin structure factor for the XXZ chain.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of charge-spin coupling, describes the Kitaev-Hubbard model and numerical methods (CPT/ED), presents results for the Z chain, XY chain, and two-leg ladder, and concludes with the implications for spectroscopic probes of spin liquids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. 12-site clusters used in our calculations. (a) Single chain extracted from the honeycomb lattice. Black dots rep- resent lattice sites, red lines represent x-type NN bonds, and green lines represent y-type NN bonds. (b) A two-leg ladder extracted from the honeycomb lattice. Both solid blue lines and dashed blue lines represent z-type bonds. (c) A two-leg ladder extracted from the square lattice, which is topologi- cally equivalent to (b).

FIG. 2. Spectral functions of the Z chain (U = 3). Panels (a)–(c) and (d)–(f) correspond to systems doped with a single spin-down and spin-up hole, respectively, for Kitaev-like hopping amplitudes t′ = 0.5, 0.8, and 0.99.

FIG. 3. Energy gap of the in-gap states for the single-hole- doped Z chain at t′ = 0.99 for (a) a spin-down hole and (b) a spin-up hole, plotted as a function of the inverse interac- tion strength 1/U. The green line indicates the spin gap of the Ising spin chain. The gap values are extracted from the frequency-resolved spectral intensity at momentum k = π.

FIG. 4. Spectral functions of the XY chain (U = 5). Panel (a) shows the half-filled system at t′ = 0. Panels (b)–(d) display the single-hole-doped system for Kitaev-like hopping amplitudes t′ = 0.0, 0.7, and 0.99, respectively. Panel (e) provides a magnified view of the energy window in (d) to clearly resolve the dispersion of the in-gap states. Panel (f) plots the transverse spin structure factor of the corresponding effective spin chain at t′ = 1 (J = 0, K = 2t′2/U = 2/5), calculated via ED on a 24-site cluster.

FIG. 5. Spectral functions of the two-leg ladder (U = 5). Panel (a) shows the half-filled system at t′ = 0. Panels (b)– (d) display the spectra for the single-hole-doped system with t′ = 0.0, 0.5, and 0.99, respectively. Panel (e) provides a magnified view of the energy window in (d) to resolve the continuum nature of the in-gap states. Panel (f) plots the transverse spin structure factor of the corresponding effective spin ladder at t′ = 1 (J = 0, K = 2t′2/U = 2/5), calculated via ED on a 2 × 12 cluster.
